				<div id="bodyContent" class="vector-body ve-init-mw-desktopArticleTarget-targetContainer" aria-labelledby="firstHeading" data-mw-ve-target-container="">
					<div class="vector-body-before-content">
							<div class="mw-indicators">
		</div>

						<div id="siteSub" class="noprint">From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia</div>
					</div>
					<div id="contentSub"><div id="mw-content-subtitle"></div></div>
					
					
					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Sequence of frequencies</div>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Harmonic_partials_on_strings.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Harmonic_partials_on_strings.svg/250px-Harmonic_partials_on_strings.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="238" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Harmonic_partials_on_strings.svg/500px-Harmonic_partials_on_strings.svg.png 1.5x" data-file-width="620" data-file-height="590"></a><figcaption>Harmonics of a string showing the periods of the pure-tone harmonics (period = 1/frequency)</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <b>harmonic series</b> (also <b>overtone series</b>) is the sequence of <a href="/wiki/Harmonics" class="mw-redirect" title="Harmonics">harmonics</a>, <a href="/wiki/Musical_tone" title="Musical tone">musical tones</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Pure_tone" title="Pure tone">pure tones</a> whose <a href="/wiki/Frequency" title="Frequency">frequency</a> is an <a href="/wiki/Integer" title="Integer">integer</a> multiple of a <i><a href="/wiki/Fundamental_frequency" title="Fundamental frequency">fundamental frequency</a></i>.
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Definite_pitch" class="mw-redirect" title="Definite pitch">Pitched</a> <a href="/wiki/Musical_instrument" title="Musical instrument">musical instruments</a> are often based on an <a href="/wiki/Acoustics" title="Acoustics">acoustic</a> <a href="/wiki/Resonator" title="Resonator">resonator</a> such as a <a href="/wiki/String_(music)" title="String (music)">string</a> or a column of air, which <a href="/wiki/Oscillation" title="Oscillation">oscillates</a> at numerous <a href="/wiki/Normal_mode" title="Normal mode">modes</a> simultaneously. As waves travel in both directions along the string or air column, they reinforce and cancel one another to form <a href="/wiki/Standing_wave" title="Standing wave">standing waves</a>. Interaction with the surrounding air produces audible <a href="/wiki/Sound_waves" class="mw-redirect" title="Sound waves">sound waves</a>, which travel away from the instrument. These frequencies are generally integer multiples, or <a href="/wiki/Harmonic" title="Harmonic">harmonics</a>, of the <a href="/wiki/Fundamental_frequency" title="Fundamental frequency">fundamental</a> and such multiples form the <a href="/wiki/Harmonic_series_(mathematics)" title="Harmonic series (mathematics)">harmonic series</a>. 
</p><p>The fundamental, which is usually perceived as the lowest <a href="#Partial">partial</a> present, is generally perceived as the <a href="/wiki/Pitch_(music)" title="Pitch (music)">pitch</a> of a musical tone. The musical <a href="/wiki/Timbre" title="Timbre">timbre</a> of a steady tone from such an instrument is strongly affected by the relative strength of each harmonic.
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Terminology">Terminology <span class="anchor" id="Partial"></span></h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Harmonic_series_(music)&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: Terminology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Partial,_harmonic,_fundamental,_inharmonicity,_and_overtone"><span id="Partial.2C_harmonic.2C_fundamental.2C_inharmonicity.2C_and_overtone"></span>Partial, harmonic, fundamental, inharmonicity, and overtone</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Harmonic_series_(music)&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Partial, harmonic, fundamental, inharmonicity, and overtone"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>A "complex tone" (the sound of a note with a timbre particular to the instrument playing the note) "can be described as a combination of many simple <a href="/wiki/Wave" title="Wave">periodic waves</a> (i.e., <a href="/wiki/Sine_wave" title="Sine wave">sine waves</a>) or <i>partials</i>, each with its own frequency of <a href="/wiki/Vibration" title="Vibration">vibration</a>, <a href="/wiki/Amplitude" title="Amplitude">amplitude</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Phase_(waves)" title="Phase (waves)">phase</a>".<sup id="cite_ref-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> (See also, <a href="/wiki/Fourier_analysis" title="Fourier analysis">Fourier analysis</a>.)
</p><p>A <i>partial</i> is any of the <a href="/wiki/Sine_wave" title="Sine wave">sine waves</a> (or "simple tones", as <a href="/wiki/Alexander_John_Ellis" title="Alexander John Ellis">Ellis</a> calls them<sup id="cite_ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> when translating <a href="/wiki/Hermann_von_Helmholtz" title="Hermann von Helmholtz">Helmholtz</a>) of which a complex tone is composed, not necessarily with an integer multiple of the lowest harmonic.
</p><p>A <i>harmonic</i> is any member of the harmonic series, an ideal set of <a href="/wiki/Frequency" title="Frequency">frequencies</a> that are <a href="/wiki/Natural_number" title="Natural number">positive integer</a> multiples of a common <a href="/wiki/Fundamental_frequency" title="Fundamental frequency">fundamental frequency</a>. The <i>fundamental</i> is a <a href="/wiki/Harmonic" title="Harmonic">harmonic</a> because it is one times itself. A <i>harmonic partial</i> is any real partial component of a complex tone that matches (or nearly matches) an ideal harmonic.<sup id="cite_ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>An <i>inharmonic partial</i> is any partial that does not match an ideal harmonic. <i><a href="/wiki/Inharmonicity" title="Inharmonicity">Inharmonicity</a></i> is a measure of the deviation of a partial from the closest ideal harmonic, typically measured in <a href="/wiki/Cent_(music)" title="Cent (music)">cents</a> for each partial.<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many <a href="/wiki/Definite_pitch" class="mw-redirect" title="Definite pitch">pitched</a> <a href="/wiki/Acoustic_music" title="Acoustic music">acoustic instruments</a> are designed to have partials that are close to being whole-number ratios with very low inharmonicity; therefore, in <a href="/wiki/Music_theory" title="Music theory">music theory</a>, and in instrument design, it is convenient, although not strictly accurate, to speak of the partials in those instruments' sounds as "harmonics", even though they may have some degree of inharmonicity. The <a href="/wiki/Piano" title="Piano">piano</a>, one of the most important instruments of western tradition, contains a certain degree of inharmonicity among the frequencies generated by each string. Other pitched instruments, especially certain <a href="/wiki/Percussion" class="mw-redirect" title="Percussion">percussion</a> instruments, such as <a href="/wiki/Marimba" title="Marimba">marimba</a>, <a href="/wiki/Vibraphone" title="Vibraphone">vibraphone</a>, <a href="/wiki/Tubular_bell" class="mw-redirect" title="Tubular bell">tubular bells</a>, <a href="/wiki/Timpani" title="Timpani">timpani</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Singing_bowl" class="mw-redirect" title="Singing bowl">singing bowls</a> contain mostly inharmonic partials, yet may give the ear a good sense of pitch because of a few strong partials that resemble harmonics. Unpitched, or indefinite-pitched instruments, such as <a href="/wiki/Cymbal" title="Cymbal">cymbals</a> and <a href="/wiki/Gong#Chau_gong_(tam-tam)" title="Gong">tam-tams</a> make sounds (produce spectra) that are rich in inharmonic partials and may give no impression of implying any particular pitch.
</p><p>An <i><a href="/wiki/Overtone" title="Overtone">overtone</a></i> is any partial above the lowest partial. The term overtone does not imply harmonicity or inharmonicity and has no other special meaning other than to exclude the fundamental. It is mostly the relative strength of the different overtones that give an instrument its particular <a href="/wiki/Timbre" title="Timbre">timbre</a>, tone color, or character. When writing or speaking of overtones and partials numerically, care must be taken to designate each correctly to avoid any confusion of one for the other, so the second overtone may not be the third partial, because it is the second sound in a series.<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some <a href="/wiki/Electronic_musical_instrument" title="Electronic musical instrument">electronic instruments</a>, such as <a href="/wiki/Synthesizer" title="Synthesizer">synthesizers</a>, can play a pure frequency with no <a href="/wiki/Overtone" title="Overtone">overtones</a> (a <a href="/wiki/Sine_wave" title="Sine wave">sine wave</a>). Synthesizers can also combine pure frequencies into more complex tones, such as to simulate other instruments. Certain flutes and ocarinas are very nearly without overtones.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Frequencies,_wavelengths,_and_musical_intervals_in_example_systems"><span id="Frequencies.2C_wavelengths.2C_and_musical_intervals_in_example_systems"></span>Frequencies, wavelengths, and musical intervals in example systems</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Harmonic_series_(music)&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Frequencies, wavelengths, and musical intervals in example systems"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Harmonic_series_to_32.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Harmonic_series_to_32.png/250px-Harmonic_series_to_32.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="333" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Harmonic_series_to_32.png/500px-Harmonic_series_to_32.png 1.5x" data-file-width="1296" data-file-height="1728"></a><figcaption>Even-numbered string harmonics from 2nd up to the 64th (five octaves)</figcaption></figure>
<p>One of the simplest cases to visualise is a <a href="/wiki/String_vibration" title="String vibration">vibrating string</a>, as in the illustration; the string has fixed points at each end, and each harmonic <a href="/wiki/Normal_mode" title="Normal mode">mode</a> divides it into an integer number (1, 2, 3, 4, etc.) of equal-sized sections resonating at increasingly higher frequencies.<sup id="cite_ref-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability" title="Wikipedia:Verifiability"><span title="The material near this tag failed verification of its source citation(s). (October 2022)">failed verification</span></a></i>]</sup> Similar arguments apply to vibrating air columns in <a href="/wiki/Wind_instrument" title="Wind instrument">wind instruments</a> (for example, "the French <a href="/wiki/Horn_(instrument)" title="Horn (instrument)">horn</a> was originally a valveless instrument that could play only the notes of the harmonic series"<sup id="cite_ref-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>), although these are complicated by having the possibility of anti-nodes (that is, the air column is closed at one end and open at the other), <a href="/wiki/Cone_(geometry)" class="mw-redirect" title="Cone (geometry)">conical</a> as opposed to <a href="/wiki/Cylinder_(geometry)" class="mw-redirect" title="Cylinder (geometry)">cylindrical</a> <a href="/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments)" title="Bore (wind instruments)">bores</a>, or end-openings that run the <a href="/wiki/Diatonic_and_chromatic" title="Diatonic and chromatic">gamut</a> from no flare, cone flare, or exponentially shaped flares (such as in various bells).
</p><p>In most pitched musical instruments, the fundamental (first harmonic) is accompanied by other, higher-frequency harmonics. Thus shorter-wavelength, higher-frequency <a href="/wiki/Wave" title="Wave">waves</a> occur with varying prominence and give each instrument its characteristic <a href="/wiki/Timbre" title="Timbre">tone quality</a>. The fact that a string is fixed at each end means that the longest allowed wavelength on the string (which gives the fundamental frequency) is twice the length of the string (one round trip, with a half cycle fitting between the nodes at the two ends). Other allowed wavelengths are reciprocal multiples (e.g. <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1154941027">.mw-parser-output .frac{white-space:nowrap}.mw-parser-output .frac .num,.mw-parser-output .frac .den{font-size:80%;line-height:0;vertical-align:super}.mw-parser-output .frac .den{vertical-align:sub}.mw-parser-output .sr-only{border:0;clip:rect(0,0,0,0);clip-path:polygon(0px 0px,0px 0px,0px 0px);height:1px;margin:-1px;overflow:hidden;padding:0;position:absolute;width:1px}</style><span class="frac"><span class="num">1</span>⁄<span class="den">2</span></span>, <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1154941027"><span class="frac"><span class="num">1</span>⁄<span class="den">3</span></span>, <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1154941027"><span class="frac"><span class="num">1</span>⁄<span class="den">4</span></span> times) that of the fundamental.
</p><p>Theoretically, these shorter wavelengths correspond to <a href="/wiki/Vibration" title="Vibration">vibrations</a> at frequencies that are integer multiples of (e.g. 2, 3, 4 times) the fundamental frequency. Physical characteristics of the vibrating medium and/or the resonator it vibrates against often alter these frequencies. (See <a href="/wiki/Inharmonicity" title="Inharmonicity">inharmonicity</a> and <a href="/wiki/Stretched_tuning" title="Stretched tuning">stretched tuning</a> for alterations specific to wire-stringed instruments and certain <a href="/wiki/Electric_piano" title="Electric piano">electric pianos</a>.) However, those alterations are small, and except for precise, highly specialized tuning, it is reasonable to think of the frequencies of the harmonic series as integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.
</p><p>The harmonic series is an <a href="/wiki/Arithmetic_progression" title="Arithmetic progression">arithmetic progression</a> (<i>f</i>, 2<i>f</i>, 3<i>f</i>, 4<i>f</i>, 5<i>f</i>, ...). In terms of frequency (measured in <a href="/wiki/Cycle_per_second" title="Cycle per second">cycles per second</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Hertz" title="Hertz">hertz</a>, where <i>f</i> is the fundamental frequency), the difference between consecutive harmonics is therefore constant and equal to the fundamental. But because human ears respond to sound <a href="/wiki/Nonlinear_system" title="Nonlinear system">nonlinearly</a>, higher harmonics are perceived as "closer together" than lower ones. On the other hand, the <a href="/wiki/Octave" title="Octave">octave</a> series is a <a href="/wiki/Geometric_progression" title="Geometric progression">geometric progression</a> (2<i>f</i>, 4<i>f</i>, 8<i>f</i>, 16<i>f</i>, ...), and people perceive these distances as "<a href="/wiki/Octave#Equivalence" title="Octave">the same</a>" in the sense of <a href="/wiki/Interval_(music)" title="Interval (music)">musical interval</a>. In terms of what one hears, each successively higher <a href="/wiki/Octave" title="Octave">octave</a> in the harmonic series is divided into increasingly "smaller" and more numerous intervals.
</p><p>The second harmonic, whose frequency is twice the fundamental, sounds an octave higher; the third harmonic, three times the frequency of the fundamental, sounds a <a href="/wiki/Perfect_fifth" title="Perfect fifth">perfect fifth</a> above the second harmonic. The fourth harmonic vibrates at four times the frequency of the fundamental and sounds a <a href="/wiki/Perfect_fourth" title="Perfect fourth">perfect fourth</a> above the third harmonic (two octaves above the fundamental). Double the harmonic number means double the frequency (which sounds an octave higher).
</p>
<figure class="mw-halign-center" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Harmonic_Series.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Harmonic_Series.png/960px-Harmonic_Series.png" decoding="async" width="650" height="88" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Harmonic_Series.png/1280px-Harmonic_Series.png 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Harmonic_Series.png/1920px-Harmonic_Series.png 2x" data-file-width="2094" data-file-height="285"></a><figcaption>An illustration in musical notation of the harmonic series (on C) up to the 20th harmonic. The numbers above the harmonic indicate the difference – in <a href="/wiki/Cent_(music)" title="Cent (music)">cents</a> – from <a href="/wiki/Equal_temperament" title="Equal temperament">equal temperament</a> (rounded to the nearest integer). Blue notes are very flat and red notes are very sharp. Listeners accustomed to more <a href="/wiki/Consonance_and_dissonance" title="Consonance and dissonance">tonal</a> tuning, such as <a href="/wiki/Meantone_temperament" title="Meantone temperament">meantone</a> and <a href="/wiki/Well_temperament" title="Well temperament">well temperaments</a>, notice many other notes are "off".</figcaption></figure>

These are "<a href="/wiki/Prime_number" title="Prime number">prime</a> harmonics".<sup id="cite_ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><span class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File"><span><span class="mw-tmh-player audio mw-file-element" style="width:250px;"><audio id="mwe_player_1_placeholder" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="" width="250" style="width:250px;" data-mwtitle="Notation_of_partials_1-19_for_1-1.mid" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" playsinline="" disabled="disabled" tabindex="-1"></audio><a class="mw-tmh-play" href="/wiki/File:Notation_of_partials_1-19_for_1-1.mid" title="Play audio" role="button"><span class="mw-tmh-play-icon notheme"></span></a><span class="mw-tmh-duration mw-tmh-label"><span class="sr-only">Duration: 0 seconds.</span><span aria-hidden="true">0:00</span></span></span></span></span></figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Marin_Mersenne" title="Marin Mersenne">Marin Mersenne</a> wrote: "The order of the Consonances is natural, and ... the way we count them, starting from unity up to the number six and beyond is founded in nature."<sup id="cite_ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, to quote <a href="/wiki/Carl_Dahlhaus" title="Carl Dahlhaus">Carl Dahlhaus</a>, "the interval-distance of the natural-tone-row [<a href="/wiki/Overtone" title="Overtone">overtones</a>] [...], counting up to 20, includes everything from the octave to the quarter tone, (and) useful and useless musical tones. The natural-tone-row [harmonic series] justifies everything, that means, nothing."<sup id="cite_ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Harmonics_and_tuning">Harmonics and tuning</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Harmonic_series_(music)&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Harmonics and tuning"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>If the harmonics are octave displaced and compressed into the span of one <a href="/wiki/Octave" title="Octave">octave</a>, some of them are approximated by the notes of what the <a href="/wiki/Western_world" title="Western world">West</a> has adopted as the chromatic scale based on the fundamental tone. The Western chromatic scale has been modified into twelve equal <a href="/wiki/Minor_second" class="mw-redirect" title="Minor second">semitones</a>, which is slightly out of tune with many of the harmonics, especially the 7th, 11th, and 13th harmonics. In the late 1930s, composer <a href="/wiki/Paul_Hindemith" title="Paul Hindemith">Paul Hindemith</a> ranked musical intervals according to their relative <a href="/wiki/Consonance_and_dissonance" title="Consonance and dissonance">dissonance</a> based on these and similar harmonic relationships.<sup id="cite_ref-Hindemith_11-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hindemith-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The table below compares the frequencies of the first 31 harmonics to their nearest equivalents in <a href="/wiki/12-tone_equal_temperament" class="mw-redirect" title="12-tone equal temperament">12-tone equal temperament</a>(12TET), normalized to one octave. Tinted fields highlight differences greater than 5 <a href="/wiki/Cent_(music)" title="Cent (music)">cents</a> (<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1154941027"><span class="frac"><span class="num">1</span>⁄<span class="den">20</span></span> of a semitone), which is the human ear's "<a href="/wiki/Just_noticeable_difference" class="mw-redirect" title="Just noticeable difference">just noticeable difference</a>" for notes played one after the other (smaller differences are noticeable with notes played simultaneously).
</p>

<p>The frequencies of the harmonic series, being integer multiples of the fundamental frequency, are naturally related to each other by whole-numbered ratios and small whole-numbered ratios are likely the basis of the consonance of musical intervals (see <a href="/wiki/Just_intonation" title="Just intonation">just intonation</a>). This objective structure is augmented by psychoacoustic phenomena. For example, a perfect fifth, say 200 and 300 Hz (cycles per second), causes a listener to perceive a <a href="/wiki/Combination_tone" title="Combination tone">combination tone</a> of 100 Hz (the difference between 300 Hz and 200 Hz); that is, an octave below the lower (actual sounding) note. This 100 Hz first-order combination tone then interacts with both notes of the interval to produce second-order combination tones of 200 (300 − 100) and 100 (200 − 100) Hz and all further nth-order combination tones are all the same, being formed from various subtraction of 100, 200, and 300. When one contrasts this with a dissonant interval such as a <a href="/wiki/Tritone" title="Tritone">tritone</a> (not tempered) with a frequency ratio of 7:5 one gets, for example, 700 − 500 = 200 (1st order combination tone) and 500 − 200 = 300 (2nd order). The rest of the combination tones are octaves of 100 Hz so the 7:5 interval actually contains four notes: 100 Hz (and its octaves), 300 Hz, 500 Hz and 700 Hz. The lowest combination tone (100 Hz) is a seventeenth (two octaves and a <a href="/wiki/Major_third" title="Major third">major third</a>) below the lower (actual sounding) note of the <a href="/wiki/Tritone" title="Tritone">tritone</a>. All the intervals succumb to similar analysis as has been demonstrated by <a href="/wiki/Paul_Hindemith" title="Paul Hindemith">Paul Hindemith</a> in his book <i>The Craft of Musical Composition</i>, although he rejected the use of harmonics from the seventh and beyond.<sup id="cite_ref-Hindemith_11-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hindemith-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Mixolydian_mode" title="Mixolydian mode">Mixolydian mode</a> is consonant with the first 10 harmonics of the harmonic series (the 11th harmonic, a tritone, is not in the Mixolydian mode). The <a href="/wiki/Ionian_mode" title="Ionian mode">Ionian mode</a> is consonant with only the first 6 harmonics of the series (the seventh harmonic, a minor seventh, is not in the Ionian mode). The <a href="/wiki/Rishabhapriya" title="Rishabhapriya">Rishabhapriya ragam</a> is consonant with the first 14 harmonics of the series.
</p>

srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/120px-Question_book-new.svg.png 1.5x" data-file-width="512" data-file-height="399"></a></span></div></td><td class="mbox-text"><div class="mbox-text-span">This section <b>needs additional citations for <a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability" title="Wikipedia:Verifiability">verification</a></b>.<span class="hide-when-compact"> Please help <a href="/wiki/Special:EditPage/Harmonic_series_(music)" title="Special:EditPage/Harmonic series (music)">improve this article</a> by <a href="/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners" title="Help:Referencing for beginners">adding citations to reliable sources</a> in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.</span>  <span class="date-container"><i>(<span class="date">November 2011</span>)</i></span><span class="hide-when-compact"><i> (<small><a href="/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal" title="Help:Maintenance template removal">Learn how and when to remove this message</a></small>)</i></span></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>The relative <a href="/wiki/Amplitude" title="Amplitude">amplitudes</a> (strengths) of the various harmonics primarily determine the <a href="/wiki/Timbre" title="Timbre">timbre</a> of different instruments and sounds, though onset <a href="/wiki/Transient_(acoustics)" title="Transient (acoustics)">transients</a>, <a href="/wiki/Formant" title="Formant">formants</a>, <a href="/wiki/Noise" title="Noise">noises</a>, and inharmonicities also play a role. For example, the <a href="/wiki/Clarinet" title="Clarinet">clarinet</a> and <a href="/wiki/Saxophone" title="Saxophone">saxophone</a> have similar <a href="/wiki/Mouthpiece_(woodwind)" title="Mouthpiece (woodwind)">mouthpieces</a> and <a href="/wiki/Reed_(music)" class="mw-redirect" title="Reed (music)">reeds</a>, and both produce sound through <a href="/wiki/Resonance" title="Resonance">resonance</a> of air inside a chamber whose mouthpiece end is considered closed. Because the clarinet's resonator is cylindrical, the <i>even</i>-numbered harmonics are less present. The saxophone's resonator is conical, which allows the even-numbered harmonics to sound more strongly and thus produces a more complex tone. The <a href="/wiki/Inharmonicity" title="Inharmonicity">inharmonic</a> ringing of the instrument's metal resonator is even more prominent in the sounds of brass instruments.
</p><p>Human ears tend to group phase-coherent, harmonically-related frequency components into a single sensation. Rather than perceiving the individual partials–harmonic and inharmonic, of a musical tone, humans perceive them together as a tone color or timbre, and the overall <a href="/wiki/Pitch_(music)" title="Pitch (music)">pitch</a> is heard as the fundamental of the harmonic series being experienced. If a sound is heard that is made up of even just a few simultaneous sine tones, and if the intervals among those tones form part of a harmonic series, the brain tends to group this input into a sensation of the pitch of the fundamental of that series, <a href="/wiki/Missing_fundamental" title="Missing fundamental">even if the fundamental is not present</a>.
</p><p>Variations in the frequency of harmonics can also affect the <i>perceived</i> fundamental pitch. These variations, most clearly documented in the piano and other stringed instruments but also apparent in <a href="/wiki/Brass_instrument" title="Brass instrument">brass instruments</a>, are caused by a combination of metal stiffness and the interaction of the vibrating air or string with the resonating body of the instrument.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Interval_strength">Interval strength</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Harmonic_series_(music)&action=edit&section=6" title="Edit section: Interval strength"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/David_Cope" title="David Cope">David Cope</a> (1997) suggests the concept of <a href="/wiki/Interval_(music)#Consonant_and_dissonant" title="Interval (music)">interval strength</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-Cope_12-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cope-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> in which an interval's strength, consonance, or stability (see <a href="/wiki/Consonance_and_dissonance" title="Consonance and dissonance">consonance and dissonance</a>) is determined by its approximation to a lower and stronger, or higher and weaker, position in the harmonic series. See also: <a href="/wiki/Lipps%E2%80%93Meyer_law" title="Lipps–Meyer law">Lipps–Meyer law</a>.
</p><p>Thus, an equal-tempered perfect fifth (<span class="ext-phonos"><span data-nosnippet="" id="ooui-php-1" class="noexcerpt ext-phonos-PhonosButton oo-ui-widget oo-ui-widget-enabled </p>
